skill.name: fixing-accessibility
skill.description: Review UI work for common accessibility problems and propose minimal, targeted fixes.
skill.summary: Find and fix accessibility issues in interface code and design changes.
skill.author: ibelick

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged slash-command examples, the word description, aria-describedby guidance, and accessibility text as risky patterns. Review found these are documentation instructions, not executable code or prompt injection. No evidence found of malicious intent, data exfiltration, unsafe command execution, or hidden tool abuse.

content.value_statement: Accessibility defects can make interfaces difficult or impossible to use. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused checklist for finding issues and suggesting small fixes.
actual_capabilities: Checks interactive controls for accessible names and labels.
Reviews keyboard access, focus visibility, and dialog behavior.
Identifies semantic HTML problems and unnecessary ARIA usage.
Flags form error, helper text, and required field announcement issues.
Suggests minimal code-level fixes instead of broad UI rewrites.
limitations: It is a guidance-only skill and does not run automated accessibility tests.
It cannot verify real screen reader behavior without a test environment.
It depends on the user providing relevant UI code or context.
It does not replace WCAG conformance review by an accessibility specialist.

use_cases.title: Review a React component
use_cases.description: Find missing labels, weak semantics, and keyboard access gaps before merging UI code.

use_cases.title: Check a form flow
use_cases.description: Improve labels, helper text, error messages, and invalid state announcements in a form.

use_cases.title: Audit a design implementation
use_cases.description: Compare a finished interface against core accessibility rules for focus, contrast, and interaction states.

prompt_templates.title: Basic accessibility review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want a quick check of one UI file.
prompt_templates.prompt: /fixing-accessibility Review this component for accessibility issues. List violations, why each matters, and a minimal fix.

prompt_templates.title: Form accessibility check
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when reviewing labels, errors, helper text, and validation states.
prompt_templates.prompt: /fixing-accessibility Review this form for labels, required fields, helper text, aria-describedby, aria-invalid, and error announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Keyboard and dialog review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when checking menus, dialogs, overlays, and custom controls.
prompt_templates.prompt: /fixing-accessibility Review this interaction for keyboard access, focus management, Escape behavior, and visible focus states.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced implementation audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Use before release when you need prioritized findings and concrete changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: /fixing-accessibility Audit these UI changes. Prioritize critical issues first, quote the relevant snippets, and propose minimal code-level fixes.

output_examples.input: An icon-only close button has no accessible name.
output_examples.output: Violation: the button has no accessible name. Why it matters: screen reader users cannot identify the action. Fix: add a clear label such as Close dialog.

output_examples.input: A modal opens without moving focus inside it.
output_examples.output: Violation: focus is not managed when the dialog opens. Why it matters: keyboard users may remain behind the overlay. Fix: move focus to the dialog title or first actionable control.

output_examples.input: A form error appears visually but is not linked to the input.
output_examples.output: Violation: the error message is not associated with the field. Why it matters: assistive technology may not announce the problem. Fix: connect the message to the input with aria-describedby and set aria-invalid.

best_practices: Provide the relevant component, template, or interaction code for review.
Ask for prioritized findings when several accessibility areas may be affected.
Apply small fixes first, then verify behavior with keyboard and assistive technology testing.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to rewrite unrelated UI code during an accessibility pass.
Do not add ARIA roles where native HTML elements already provide correct semantics.
Do not treat checklist review as proof of full WCAG compliance.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews UI accessibility basics such as labels, keyboard access, focus behavior, semantics, forms, announcements, contrast, and media.

faq.question: Does it run automated accessibility tools?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and suggested fixes, but it does not execute scanners or browser tests.

faq.question: Can it fix code directly?
faq.answer: Yes, when used in a coding tool with file access, it can suggest or apply small targeted fixes.

faq.question: Is it only for React?
faq.answer: No. The rules are general and apply to HTML, component frameworks, and design system code.

faq.question: Does it guarantee WCAG compliance?
faq.answer: No. It helps catch common issues, but formal compliance requires broader testing and expert review.

faq.question: How should I use it before release?
faq.answer: Provide the changed UI files and ask for prioritized violations, impact, and minimal code-level fixes.
